content.full_text:
State of California

Memorandum

To    : File                                                Date : Feb. 25, 1977

                                                            77-047/121

From  : FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
         Ken Finney

Subject: Telephone conversation with Mr. Kline, an attorney for a
         Los Angeles County Superior Court Judge.

Mr. Kline, an attorney representing a Los Angeles County Superior
Court Judge, called to ask a number of questions with respect to the
judge's statement of Economic Interests. First, he asked what the period covered was and I informed him that it was May 5, 1976, to December 31, 1976. Second, he inquired whether the repayment of a loan had to be reported as income because it was a return of capital, and I responded that it did have to be reported as income. Third, he asked whether a personal loan made by the filer's spouse was an investment of the spouse. I responded that a personal loan was not an investment. Fourth, he inquired about how to reveal a sale of real property. I responded that he must show the interest in real property and that it was disposed of and that he also should report the income received from the sale of the property. Fifth, he inquired about the disclosure obligations created by an investment in a partnership which in turn has interests in real property. I responded that if the investment in the partnership is worth more than $1,000, it must be revealed; and, in turn, if the investment in the partnership represents a 1% ownership interest or greater, the pro rata share of the interests in real property BY THE PARTNERSHIP also must be revealed. Sixth, he inquired about disclosure of the clients of the filer's spouse, who is an attorney. I responded by reciting the reasonable diligence standard that we have developed to him. In other words, he must inquire of the spouse and if the spouse refuses to divulge the identity of the clients, he nevertheless must disclose those clients which he knows or has reason to know about. Finally, he wondered whether the income reporting obligations are based on gross receipts and hence create a reporting obligation even if there is a net loss. I responded that there can be a reporting obligation if a payment is received even if it involves a net loss.

KF:jo

sections.question_synthetic: An attorney representing a Los Angeles County Superior Court Judge called with multiple questions regarding the Judge's Statement of Economic Interests, including reporting requirements for loan repayments, spouse's personal loans, real property sales, partnership investments, spouse's client disclosures, and income reporting obligations.
sections.conclusion_synthetic: The FPPC provided guidance on seven specific matters: (1) loan repayments must be reported as income; (2) personal loans from a spouse are not investments; (3) real property sales require disclosure of the interest and income; (4) partnership investments over $1,000 must be revealed, with pro rata real property interests disclosed if ownership is 1% or greater; (5) spouse's clients must be disclosed to the extent known or reasonably ascertainable; and (6) income reporting obligations apply even when resulting in a net loss.
sections.parsing_notes: No section headers found; LLM: Document is a memorandum of a telephone conversation rather than a formal advice letter with traditional Q/C sections. Content consists of narrative summary of questions asked and responses provided by FPPC staff. Some text degradation in OCR affecting readability of certain passages.

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: An attorney representing a Los Angeles County Superior Court Judge called with multiple questions regarding the Judge's Statement of Economic Interests, including reporting requirements for loan repayments, spouse's personal loans, real property sales, partnership investments, spouse's client disclosures, and income reporting obligations.

CONCLUSION: The FPPC provided guidance on seven specific matters: (1) loan repayments must be reported as income; (2) personal loans from a spouse are not investments; (3) real property sales require disclosure of the interest and income; (4) partnership investments over $1,000 must be revealed, with pro rata real property interests disclosed if ownership is 1% or greater; (5) spouse's clients must be disclosed to the extent known or reasonably ascertainable; and (6) income reporting obligations apply even when resulting in a net loss.

embedding.summary: Memorandum documenting a telephone conversation between FPPC staff member Ken Pinney and attorney Mr. Kline representing a Los Angeles County Superior Court Judge, in which the FPPC provided guidance on seven issues related to Statement of Economic Interests reporting requirements.
